title: How to Work with Recurring Visions and Symbolic Content
body:
1. That Moment When the Symbols Keep Coming Back

I still remember the first time a vision kept revisiting me after a ceremony. It wasn’t just a flash or something fleeting on the edges of my mind. No, it was a striking geometric pattern, like a glowing mandala, hovering and shifting just behind my eyelids every time I closed them. At first, I shrugged it off, thinking, “Probably just my brain playing tricks or leftover effects of the mushrooms.” But as days turned into weeks and this same symbol surfaced during meditation, dreams, and even random moments in the day, it started to gnaw at me. What was it trying to say? Why did it keep showing up? Was this a message, a warning, or just noise?

These recurring visions, especially when they come loaded with symbolic content, have tripped me up more than once. You see, coming back to these motifs can either feel like a comforting guide or an unsettling puzzle. And sometimes, it’s both at once. As someone who has sat with mushroom visions since the early days of my personal journey and with the stories of countless others over 15 years, I can say this: recurring visions aren’t just random. They often carry a deeper meaning waiting for your engagement. Yet, the question is how do you work with them instead of pushing them away or getting stuck in confusion?

It’s tempting to dismiss these visions as hallucinations or to get overly literal and try to “decode” them with a quick Google search. But in psychedelic therapy and mushroom medicine, these symbols often reflect inner states, unresolved emotions, or pathways your subconscious is inviting you to explore. They are the brain’s way of communicating through a language that’s less about words and more about feeling and intuition. Think of them as emotional hieroglyphs, deeply personal yet surprisingly universal.

Throughout my years, I’ve seen people ignore these signals, missing out on deep growth, while others dive headlong without enough support and get overwhelmed. So if you’re sitting with recurring visions, know that you’re in a sacred zone - a place where your psyche is knocking to be heard. The challenge and the gift both lie in deciphering these symbols with patience, curiosity, and the right tools. What follows in this article is a guide - one I wish I had back then. We’ll explore what these visions mean, the science behind them, how to work practically with recurring symbolic content, and how to weave them into your everyday life without losing your footing.

2. Understanding Recurring Visions and Symbolic Content: The Brain’s Hidden Language

To make sense of why certain visions recur and why symbols seem to pull us back like magnets, we need to start by talking about how the brain processes psychedelic experiences. Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind gives a brilliant window into this, explaining how substances like psilocybin temporarily disrupt the default mode network (DMN) - the part of the brain responsible for our ego and habitual thought patterns.

When the DMN quiets down, it’s like the walls inside our mind come tumbling down. This allows other networks to communicate more freely, especially those tied to memory, emotion, and sensory perception. The result is a flood of imagery and symbols arising from deep within the subconscious. These symbols aren’t just random hallucinations; they’re the brain’s way of processing complex emotional material, sometimes trauma, sometimes unresolved conflicts, sometimes personal mythologies. Stanislav Grof, one of the early pioneers in psychedelic research, referred to this process as “spiritual emergency” - a breakdown that can lead to a breakthrough if navigated wisely.

Neuroscience supports this idea. Research led by Robin Carhart-Harris and his team at Imperial College London showed that psychedelics reduce activity in the DMN but increase communication between normally disconnected regions of the brain. This “cross-talk” creates a rich ground for symbolic content to emerge. Symbols that recur might be your subconscious’s way of telling you something unresolved keeps knocking, needing to be witnessed and understood. Sometimes these images are archetypal - patterns humanity has carried for millennia, like spirals, mandalas, or serpents. Other times, they are deeply personal - snippets from your past, childhood memories, or emotional wounds. Either way, these symbols serve as a bridge between the conscious and unconscious, a kind of dream language given vivid form.

Neurochemically, psychedelics increase serotonin receptor activity, particularly at the 5-HT2A receptor, which modulates brain circuits linked to cognition and perception. This receptor site is densely packed in areas associated with vision and imagination. So when this system is activated, the brain’s natural storytelling machinery kicks into overdrive, weaving symbolic narratives from fragments of memory, emotion, and sensory input. That’s why you might see the same symbol repeatedly: your brain is using it as a key to open a deeper layer of experience. These repetitions aren’t glitches, but clues.

Understanding that recurring visions are your brain’s language in flux can free you from the fear of “losing your mind.” It’s simply your psyche speaking a different dialect, one you can learn to listen to more fluently over time.

3. What Science Says About Recurring Visions: Studies and Stories

There’s growing interest among scientists in the meaningfulness of symbolic content during psychedelic experiences. One landmark study by Roland Griffiths and colleagues at Johns Hopkins University found that participants who reported “mystical-type experiences” during psilocybin sessions had lasting positive changes in mood and behavior. Importantly, many of these mystical experiences included symbolic visions - visions imbued with personal and universal meaning.

Griffiths’ work emphasized that the content of the visions often correlated with changes in anxiety, depression, and even addiction. In other words, the symbols weren’t just pretty images; they seemed to operate as therapeutic agents. Returning to a symbol in multiple sessions or afterwards during integration was linked with deeper psychological shifts. This suggests that sustained engagement with symbolic material can help rewire the brain’s emotional circuits.

Building on this, Carhart-Harris’ team used fMRI scans to observe brain activity under psychedelics. They noticed that the brain’s increased entropy or “disorder” state lets hidden material bubble up to consciousness. This supports the idea that recurring symbols might be unresolved emotional or cognitive “knots” your mind is working through. As the brain’s networks rebalance post-experience, these symbols can either fade or remain, inviting further attention.

Stanislav Grof’s decades of clinical work provide a treasure trove of insights into symbolic content and recurrence. He saw many patients revisit the same symbols or visions across sessions, often tied to personal psychological archetypes or transpersonal themes like birth, death, and rebirth. He argued that these patterns are not random but part of a healing process that calls for the patient to face and integrate these experiences intentionally.

Nathaniel Dominy’s research on the evolutionary basis of psychedelic use among indigenous peoples points to the role of symbols as communication tools between humans and the natural world. This adds another dimension: symbols might connect you not only to your inner psyche but also to something larger, a “wild mind” we all carry.

From a clinical perspective, researchers like Charles Grob and Bill Richards have worked extensively with symbolic content in psychedelic sessions, recommending therapeutic approaches that include mapping symbols, journaling, and art therapy. This clinical validation underscores the importance of recurring visions as more than mere epiphenomena of intoxication - they can be guideposts toward healing.

4. Practical Ways to Work with Recurring Visions

So how do you engage with these repeating visions beyond just noticing them and moving on? From my own experience and working with others, the first step is giving yourself permission to slow down and make space for the symbols. Grab a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound or any notebook and start by writing down every detail you remember about the vision - what it looked like, how it felt, and where it pops up. Don’t censor yourself or try to “analyze” yet. Just record. This externalization helps move the symbol from elusive impression to something tangible.

Next, approach the symbol with curiosity, not judgement. Ask yourself: When else in my life have I seen something like this? What feelings or memories come up? Let the symbol be a kind of mirror or storyteller. If this is hard, guided meditation or gentle creative expression like drawing or movement can loosen up the mental grip you have on the symbol and reveal new layers.

Psychotherapist and researcher Charles Grob recommends pairing symbol exploration with somatic awareness work, which means tuning into how the symbol feels in your body. This is where books like The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk become invaluable. They teach you to notice where trauma or emotional energy resides physically. Your recurring symbols may connect directly to these somatic “memories.”

Another practical method is to create a symbolic “map.” This can mean drawing the symbol repeatedly, noting variations, or even creating a story around it. This process externalizes internal content and makes it easier to discuss with a trusted integration guide or therapist. Bringing another witness to your recurring visions can provide new perspectives and safety.

For those who are comfortable, mindfulness meditation focusing on the symbol can allow it to unfold naturally rather than force interpretation. Sit quietly, breathe deeply, and let the symbol “speak” in whatever way it wants. Sometimes the meaning comes as a feeling rather than a clear message.

Lastly, if the symbols feel overwhelming or confusing, seek out psychedelic integration practitioners who specialize in symbolic and visionary content. They can help contextualize and ground your experience, and prevent you from spinning in interpretive loops.

5. Common Challenges and How to Move Through Them

Recurring visions aren’t always friendly. Sometimes they can be disturbing, frightening, or confusing. I’ve met many folks who get stuck on a particular symbol or vision that won’t let go - almost like a haunting. This experience can create anxiety, self-doubt, or even paranoia. This is where many people get lost or scared, sometimes calling it a “bad trip” or “psychotic break.” But it’s usually a form of resistance or a sign that the symbol is pointing to something tough.

One common challenge is trying to force meaning or closure too quickly. If you push too hard, the symbol can harden into a fixed idea, trapping you in a repetitive cycle. Instead, try to soften your approach and allow the symbol to have its own timing. Integration is rarely linear; sometimes you need to sit with ambiguity and discomfort without clear answers.

Another issue is isolation. If you’re dealing with heavy symbolic visions alone, it’s easy to get overwhelmed. That’s why community and professional support matter. Shamans, therapists like Bill Richards, or trusted peers who understand the psychedelic world can provide grounding and help you hold space for difficult material.

Physical discomfort or somatic blockages are another hurdle. Your body might react strongly to certain symbolic content, manifesting as tension, nausea, or restlessness. Techniques like breathwork, yoga, or even light exercise can help move this stuck energy and prevent it from becoming overwhelming.

Lastly, it’s easy to confuse your own symbolism with external messages or delusions. Staying grounded with reality checks and remembering that these symbols are internal guides - not literal truths - is crucial. Journaling your experiences and reading sober reflections by authors like Pollan or van der Kolk can help remind you that this is a process of self-discovery, not mystical mind control.

6. Integration Into Daily Life: A Slow Dance

Recurring visions and symbolic content don’t just disappear once the experience ends. They can linger, showing up in your dreams, your art, your relationships. Integration is the process of making these symbols part of your waking life. For me, this has been a slow, sometimes bumpy dance over years.

A practical way to integrate is by setting aside regular time for reflection. This might mean journaling in your Journaling Notebook Leather Bound, meditating on your symbol, or even working with creative outlets like painting or music. These daily reminders help the symbol morph from a mysterious vision to a living part of you.

Therapists like Bessel van der Kolk stress how trauma and emotional experiences can remain lodged in the body and mind. Working with recurring symbols can be an entry point to healing these deeper wounds - for instance, a recurring snake image might connect to fear around transformation or vulnerability. Naming this and sharing it in therapy creates new neural pathways, slowly rewiring your response.

Socially, you might find yourself drawn to communities or practices that echo your symbolic visions without even realizing it. This could be a shift toward nature, spiritual traditions, or artistic subcultures. Rather than resisting these pulls, leaning into them can enrich your sense of belonging and purpose.

Remember, integration is not about erasing the symbol but befriending it. Like an old friend who sometimes surprises you with their moods, your recurring visions become part of your ongoing conversation with yourself. This approach calms the anxiety around “what does this mean” and opens the door to “how does this serve me now.”

7. A Thought to Carry Forward

When I reflect on my years working with recurring visions and symbolic content, one thing stands out: these symbols are invitations to live more honestly with the parts of ourselves we often avoid. They are raw, unruly messengers from the depths of our being, refusing to be ignored. The question isn’t just what the symbol means, but how it challenges you to show up differently in your life.

There’s a kind of sacred persistence in symbols that recur; they are the psyche’s way of saying: “Here I am. You can’t run from this anymore.” They invite us into an ancient conversation between the known and unknown, the conscious and unconscious. And this conversation is always evolving, never static. It asks for patience, courage, and a willingness to be vulnerable.

What if recurring visions are not puzzles to solve but companions on the road? What if instead of trying to pin down their meaning, you lean into their mystery? This approach doesn’t deny intellect but invites a form of wisdom that trusts feeling and intuition alongside thought. It is less about clarity and more about presence.

As you continue on your path, I encourage you to meet your recurring symbols with kindness and openness. Carry them gently, journal their shifts, share them with trusted guides, and most importantly, allow them to teach you patience with yourself. These visions may just be the quiet medicine your soul needs to heal and grow.
